sourceTitle: A simple CRM for strengthening client relationships and managing work

questions.question: What type of Notion database layout should you choose to get an at‑a‑glance, scannable view of all client details?

options.text: Board view
options.explanation: Board view is great for Kanban pipelines, not for a simple, scannable table of all client data.

options.text: Table layout (full‑page database)
options.explanation: A full‑page Table layout displays every row and column, making client information easy to scan and edit.

options.text: Gallery view
options.explanation: Gallery view shows cards with images; it isn’t ideal for quickly reading many rows of client fields.

options.text: List view
options.explanation: List view shows one column per row and lacks the grid‑like overview a Table provides.

questions.question: Which combination of database properties would best capture a client’s primary point of contact and the team member responsible for that client?

options.text: Select for contact, Person for account manager
options.explanation: Select is for predefined tags; it wouldn’t store free‑form contact names or emails.

options.text: Text for point of contact, Person for account manager
options.explanation: Text lets you type any name or details for the contact, while Person links a workspace member as the manager.

options.text: Number for contact, Email for manager
options.explanation: Number is for numeric values; Email stores an email address, not a Notion user.

options.text: Date for contact, Multi‑select for manager
options.explanation: Date and Multi‑select don’t represent the needed data types for these fields.

questions.question: How does the article suggest you track a client’s progress through your sales funnel?

options.text: Add a Number property that increments each time the client moves forward
options.explanation: Numbers are for quantitative data, not for stage labels.

options.text: Create a Status property and a Board view grouped by that Status
options.explanation: A Status property lets you label stages (Lead, Prospect, Active, etc.) and the Board view lets you drag‑and‑drop between them.

options.text: Use a Multi‑select property for all possible stages and manually edit the list
options.explanation: Multi‑select isn’t ideal for a single current stage; Status is purpose‑built for this.

options.text: Add a Date property for each stage and sort by the most recent date
options.explanation: Dates track when something happened, not the current stage itself.

questions.question: Which tool would you use to see only the clients that are currently marked as “Active”?

options.text: Create a new Table view and apply a Filter where Status equals Active
options.explanation: Filtering a view by the Status property shows only rows that meet the “Active” condition.

options.text: Change the Table layout to a Gallery view and sort by Industry
options.explanation: Changing layout and sorting does not limit the rows to Active clients.

options.text: Add a Multi‑select tag called Active to each client
options.explanation: While possible, the article recommends using a Status property and a filter, not extra tags.

options.text: Group the Board view by Revenue
options.explanation: Grouping by Revenue does not filter by client status.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using a Chart view for a Number property that records each client’s revenue?

options.text: It automatically calculates each client’s profit margin
options.explanation: Charts visualize data; they don’t perform profit‑margin calculations unless you add a formula.

options.text: It lets you visualize revenue trends over time or by client
options.explanation: A Chart view can display monthly, quarterly, or annual revenue, helping you spot trends and compare clients.

options.text: It converts revenue numbers into currency symbols
options.explanation: Currency formatting is a property setting, not a function of the Chart view.

options.text: It creates a printable PDF report of all client revenues
options.explanation: Charts are visual components inside Notion; printing requires manual export.

questions.question: When sharing a client portal with a guest, which permission level does the article recommend for most cases?

options.text: Full edit access
options.explanation: Full edit gives guests the ability to change content, which is usually unnecessary and risky.

options.text: View and comment only
options.explanation: View + comment lets clients see information and give feedback without altering the page.

options.text: Can duplicate only
options.explanation: Duplicating isn’t a standard Notion permission; the article doesn’t mention it.

options.text: Admin rights
options.explanation: Admin rights would let guests manage sharing settings, which is not recommended.

questions.question: Which feature helps you keep client portal content consistent across multiple client pages?

options.text: Synced blocks
options.explanation: Synced blocks allow you to edit content once and have the change appear on every page where the block is used.

options.text: Database roll‑up
options.explanation: Roll‑ups aggregate data but don’t replicate page content.

options.text: Template button
options.explanation: Template buttons create new content on demand; they don’t keep existing content synchronized.

options.text: Relation property
options.explanation: Relations link records but don’t sync text or layout across pages.

questions.question: How can you streamline communication with clients directly inside the client portal?

options.text: Send all updates via external email threads
options.explanation: External email fragments communication and loses the context of the portal.

options.text: Leave comments on pages or specific blocks and tag the client with @
options.explanation: Comments keep feedback attached to the exact content and tagging notifies the client.

options.text: Create a separate Slack channel for each client
options.explanation: While possible, the article emphasizes staying inside Notion for a single source of truth.

options.text: Upload a Word document for each client update
options.explanation: Uploading documents adds friction and separates the conversation from the work.

questions.question: Which advanced Notion feature can reduce manual updates when a client’s status changes to “Live”?

options.text: Database automations that send Slack or email notifications
options.explanation: Automations can trigger notifications or other actions when a property meets a condition, removing manual steps.

options.text: AI‑generated content to rewrite the client’s description
options.explanation: AI helps draft text but does not automate status‑change notifications.

options.text: Using a Formula property to calculate the status
options.explanation: Formulas compute values; they don’t send external notifications.

options.text: Embedding a third‑party CRM via an iFrame
options.explanation: Embedding external tools isn’t mentioned as a way to automate internal Notion workflows.

questions.question: What is the recommended way to quickly set up a new client portal with consistent sections (e.g., bio, tasks, notes)?

options.text: Manually copy‑paste each section from an existing client page
options.explanation: Copy‑pasting is time‑consuming and prone to errors.

options.text: Create a New Client template within the database and populate it with the desired sections
options.explanation: A database template pre‑fills the page with the standard layout, streamlining onboarding.

options.text: Use the “Duplicate page” function after creating the first client portal
options.explanation: Duplicating works but doesn’t automatically apply to every new entry; templates are more scalable.

options.text: Add a Relation property that links to a master portal page
options.explanation: Relations link records but don’t create pre‑filled content for each client.
